learning_outcomes.description: Critically investigate and evaluate the history and contemporary cultural impact of automation
Analyse automation from  a variety of historical, philosophical, aesthetic, sociological, technological and political perspectives
Communicate effectively about the key debates and scholarship in the study of automation
Think and write critically about the cultural impact of automation on everyday life, drawing on key debates and scholarship

You must attend your tutorial to complete that week’s learning journal entry. Individual accommodations on the basis of SAPs or documentation of exceptional circumstances may be made in consultation with the Course Coordinator.
assessments.task_description:
Overview

This assessment is completed in class during 8 of your tutorials, in total. It is designed to help you reflect on your understanding of key concepts from the readings and tutorial activities, improve your ability to apply those concepts, and enhance your participation in tutorials.

You will complete structured entries in a learning journal each week during the listed weeks. Learning journal entries will be handwritten in class and collected by your tutor for assessment each week. We will reserve 15 minutes for completion of journal entries at the end of tutorials.

A total of 8 opportunities are provided to complete learning journal entries across the semester, each worth 6%. Only your best 5 results will be counted, together forming 30% of your final grade. The assessment builds in the flexibility to miss up to 3 sessions with no penalty and no documentation required.

There are no opportunities to re-take an individual learning journal entry, and no alternative assessment is available except in the specific circumstances set out below.

This is a device-free assessment classified as “Secure” under UQ’s rules for using AI in assessment: “This assessment is designed to protect academic integrity and assess your understanding of course concepts. You must only use the approved materials and tools for this assessment task. Use of prohibited materials in this task may be investigated and treated as academic misconduct.” Use of AI, electronic devices and any form of pre-prepared written or other materials are not permitted for this assessment. Journal entries can only be completed in class.

Alternative assessment and accommodations

If you have a Disability Action Plan relevant to this task, please contact the course coordinator as early as possible. Reasonable accommodations can be made for matters documented in a Disability Action Plan (for example, if you are unable to handwrite under timed conditions).

A detailed marking rubric and complete task description can be found in your Assessment 1 folder.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Your tutor will provide a printed learning journal template sheet for you to complete and submit in the tutorial. Your journal entries will be collected in class; your mark will be recorded in Gradescope.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: Digital Poster Presentation + Peer Engagement Coordination
assessments.due_date:
Week 2 - Week 12

The poster must be submitted by 4pm the day before the tutorial in which the student is presenting.
assessments.mode: Activity/ Performance, Oral, Product/ Artefact/ Multimedia
assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Presentation, Poster, Product/ Design
assessments.task_description:
Overview

This assignment has two components: a digital poster design, and a peer engagement coordination. During your first tutorial, you will be allocated a week in which to present your digital poster and coordinate peer engagement. You will submit your digital poster via the Turnitin link on Blackboard. The submission link is in your Assessment 2 folder.

For this assignment, you will be assessed on the content and quality of your digital poster and the quality of your peer engagement coordination. The rubric and complete task description is available in your Assessment 2 folder. 

During the Week 1 tutorial, you will be allocated a week in which to present your digital poster and facilitate discussion.

Length: 5-minute Digital Poster Presentation + 5-minute Peer Engagement Coordination

Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment 

  • All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device)  
  • Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course.  
  • Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of: 
  • Moderation of marking;  
  • Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or  
  • Re-marking following a successful re-mark application.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit your poster via Turnitin. The submission portal is in your Assessment 2 folder on the course Blackboard site
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Word Count: 1,500 – 2,000 words (excluding references)

Overview

In this course, we explore how cultures shape, resist, and are reconfigured by automated systems. Rather than treating automation as an abstract phenomenon external to our lives, this assessment tasks you with acting as a participant-observer within your own classroom community.

Throughout the semester, three students each week will deliver a poster presentation and coordinate peer discussions during tutorials. Your task is to track, document, and analyse these live, unrecorded exchanges.

You will select two specific tutorial sessions as your raw "field sites" and build a critical analytical report evaluating how the conceptual friction generated in those discussions reflects broader cultural concerns surrounding automated systems.

The assignment rubric and complete task description is provided in the Assessment 3 folder on Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission portal is available your Assessment 3 folder in the course Blackboard site.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.
